title: vimeo_1170158884
displayTitle: 2026-03-25 - Regulations Review Committee - Complaint about the Land Transport (Road User) Rule 2004 and the Vehicle Use and Parking Bylaw 2025 (Auckland)
topic: Complaint about the Land Transport (Road User) Rule 2004 and the Vehicle Use and Parking Bylaw 2025 (Auckland)
description: Complaint about the Land Transport (Road User) Rule 2004 and the Vehicle Use and Parking Bylaw 2025 (Auckland) Wiri Business Association Gary Holmes, General Manager Auckland Council Katy Bexley, Associate General Counsel Public Law Auckland Transport Sarah Preston, Solicitor
descriptionSummary: Complaint about the Land Transport (Road User) Rule 2004 and the Vehicle Use and Parking Bylaw 2025 (Auckland). Wiri Business Association. Gary Holmes, General Manager

chunks.text: It probably has some questions, but if you want to just take us through for a couple of minutes why you think, what the stakes are for you and your members- Sure... why you're complaining about the issue holistically, and then we've got some specific follow-ups for you. No problem. Cool. Thank you very much for the opportunity to address you. So look, I'm representing, as you know, the Wiri Business Association.

We believe we're the industrial engine of Auckland, and we're a critical hub for New Zealand's supply chain. We represent more than 700 businesses and about 600 individual property owners in the area. So I'm here for two reasons, two references. One is the Vehicle Use and Parking By-law 2025 under Auckland Transport, and also the Land Transport 2004 rule.

Essentially, the first one I'll just speak to is the Auckland Transport byelaw. Just to be fair to AT right from the outset, that Vehicle Use and Parking By-law only came into force on the 1st of July 2025, so last year. We're reasonable people. We recognize that rolling out new regulatory frameworks does take time, and we're not asking for overnight miracles.

However, the issue we're facing in Wiri, and it's also an issue in other places in Auckland and around the country, is historic and systemic. So when Parliament grants a new regulatory tool, the expectation is that it'll be deployed first where the issue is the greatest. So our concerns in relation to that particular byelaw relate to clauses 13 (1) , 13 (2) , 13 (3) , and clause 26, and they relate to the prohibiting or restricting the stopping, standing, or parking of vehicles on any road to provide for the safe operation of the road and the avoidance of

nuisance and danger. So, the byelaw gives Auckland Transport the explicit power to prohibit the stopping and parking of heavy motor vehicles to ensure safe operation and to avoid danger in that area. However, we haven't got the necessary resolutions to enforce that in Wiri. We're not talking just about daytime congestion.

We've got heavy vehicles parking in the area overnight as well, completely unlit, failing to display the legally required red lights at the back. And the Wiri Business Association has been doing the heavy lifting for Auckland Transport. So the last nine months, our security teams each night have been going around recording the vehicles that are parked without red lights, taking a photo of them, recording the location, time, and vehicle registration, and then sending it to Auckland Transport, who then, to be fair, do go on and ticket a number of those vehicles.

And I believe the number is about 2,000 infringements that have been issued in the last nine months thanks to the work that we've done in giving them that evidence on a plate. The other issue we have is clause 4, and that clause restricts the repairing or modifying of vehicles on the road or in a public place, and it restricts the dumping or discharging of fluids to help minimize public nuisance. So what is concerning for us there is the lack of resolutions and enforcement regarding roadside vehicle repairs and fluid dumping.

So our roads are effectively being used as open-air mechanic workshops. A lot of these trucks are owner-operators, and they don't have their own yards, and they just park up day and night. They're there working on their trucks and so on, repairing them, changing motors, tipping oil down the drains. We've got photographic and video evidence of that happening. So when we see these things happening, we're advising Auckland Transport immediately, so we're not just sitting there doing nothing.

The byelaw is supposed to minimize that public nuisance and that environmental damage. But without targeted resolutions in industrial areas like Wiri, the byelaw is really just ink on paper. So because the byelaw is entirely discretionary, Auckland Transport has no mandate to prioritize the areas needing the most resource. It has been nine months since the byelaw went live, but the issue goes back quite a bit further.

When this particular byelaw was being consulted upon, we, along with the Road Carriers Association of New Zealand, met with Auckland Transport representatives and highlighted the issue there. Also, just before the byelaw came into force, so in early 2025, we held a dedicated meeting with Auckland Transport's compliance parking manager...

compliance manager, sorry, and they heard directly from our members about the frustration that they're hearing. It's the number one issue that we're getting complaints about. Gary, I know you have more to say. That's all right. But could you give us a moment just for some questions to you? What, sorry? Just for some questions.

Yep. I'll come to any other members. But what I need to ask you is, how does the problem in Wiri compare to the rest of the city? And have you, as a business association manager, spoken to any other business associations who have dealt with the same problem around the country, and do they deal with it better?

So I think this is a quick first question, but in terms of other associations around Auckland, we are aware that it is an issue in other places. Mm-hmm. Even some areas that don't have business associations, it is an area. I see there was an article on Radio New Zealand yesterday about parking in some of the residential streets in Manurewa, so they are also experiencing it. But the problemOh, and sorry, also the National Road Carriers Association, we have met with them and their representatives several times about this.

It's not only just in Wery, because, of course, the Lane Transport road user rule refers to parking with due care and reasonable consideration- Mm... and covers nighttime lighting for heavy vehicles. It is clearly insufficient- Yeah... because no one is able to enforce it. So, we believe that that particular law, from a nationwide perspective, should be looked at to see where the gaps are in the legislation and how it can be made so that it can be enforced.

Briefly, let's discuss the gaps. We've only got two more minutes with you. Yep, sure. So, as I understand it, there are some rules which you think are there but not being enforced, and then there are also some rules that don't exist that should- Correct... and we'll have an opportunity to discuss that with Auckland Council and Auckland Transport in a second.

Are there a few of those gaps that you want to highlight so that we understand what you think is missing? Sorry, say that one again. What do you think is missing from the set of rules in the toolkit for you and AT to enforce? Look, I guess it's just...

I don't know how to put this into the right terms, but I guess the rules are there, but they're not being enforced, and no one's being mandated. It's sort of like when we have issues where we've got trucks parked on either side of the road. We've got other businesses that cannot get their vehicles out of their driveways because these trucks are legally parked.

We plead with Auckland Transport for things like yellow broken lines, just so we can enable these businesses to operate. Mm. And that is a mission and a half. We've managed to do it on one road, but the rest of it, it's sort of they put their hands up, and they're either not interested or don't believe it's a priority. So it's sort of making them do that.

But then when you've got that road user rule, and it says the safe operation of roads, and due consideration to care, well, clearly it's not working in our industrial area. It's not working around the country from our conversations with the National Road Carrier, and it's not working in residential areas. We've got a problem that is just getting worse.

Mm-hmm. So you've got the rules there. It's not being enforced. Awesome. There goes our time, but is it even as- That's all right. No? Okay. Thank you so much. You're welcome to stay. We will have- No, I understand... further submitters on here. Thank you. Kate from Auckland Council. You there, Kate? Pop your camera on if you are.

Katie. Katie? Hello. Kia ora. Kia ora. Thank you very much for joining the committee. We've got 10 minutes with you. Were you able to watch the submission prior to this? Great. Yes. I'm just wondering if my colleague Sarah has joined. Sarah. Yep. Is Sarah there? Yes. Sarah's joined. Thank you very much.

Awesome. Yes, we've got 10 minutes with you both. Would welcome a few minutes to talk us through the operation of the byelaw, and then we've got some questions for you as well. Sure. Kia ora koutou, and thank you for hearing from us today. So my name is Katie Bexley, and I am the Associate General Counsel of Public Law at Auckland Council. And I'm joined by my colleague, Sarah Preston, who is a solicitor in the legal team at Auckland Transport.

So we're here representing both Auckland Council and Auckland Transport today, given that the vehicle use and parking byelaw is a joint byelaw of both Auckland Council and Auckland Transport. And also because of the impending reform of transport governance in Auckland under the local government Auckland Council Transport Governance Amendment bill. It's quite a mouthful.

Which is currently progressing through Parliament, and which will see Auckland Council becoming responsible for transport-related byelaws in Auckland very soon. So we've provided our submission in writing to the committee this morning, which sets out our position in relation to the specifics of the complaint, which hopefully you will have a copy of.

But I'll just speak briefly to the key points in there, and then we're happy, of course, to take any questions. So firstly, AT and Council acknowledge the concerns raised by the Wery Business Association regarding truck parking and related activities on streets within the Wery industrial district, and the impact that such activities can have on safety, business operations, and the local environment.

However, we don't consider that there is a legislative gap or that there has been an unusual or unexpected use of power in terms of standing order 3272C. And the primary reason for that is that the byelaw gives the relevant authority, being AT or Council, as the case may be, the power to make resolutions or prohibitions for the matters set out in the byelaw.

And as mentioned, that includes in relation to stopping, standing, or parking of vehicles on any road, and with that reference to Clause 13 of the byelaw. So as noted, that power to make resolutions or prohibitions is discretionary, and that is that the byelaw empowers the making of those resolutions or prohibitions if and when the circumstances arise requiring such action. It doesn't require the making of those resolutions or prohibitions.

That approach is, of course, consistent with the empowering provisions in the Land Transport Act in Section 13 of the Bylaws Act, and there are good policy reasons for it. So as you'll be aware, Auckland is a huge area with a huge number of roads, and those roads require different controls depending on the location.

And often changes to those controls will need to be made quickly, and an approach of providing for resolutions to be made outside of the byelaw itself provides more flexibility for creating those context-specific traffic and parking rules across the region rather than having all of those rules in the byelaw itself.Safeguards are built into the process for making those resolutions, including requiring options assessments, internal and external engagement, so with local boards, affected residents and landowners, businesses,

community groups, other partners such as NZTA and the police, and of course, compliance with standard public law decision-making requirements. So it's a really robust process that is followed. And then just in terms of the lack of specific rules around preventing roadside vehicle repairs that is referenced in the complaint, we just note that Auckland Transport and Council do have other bylaws regulating these activities, and that includes AT's activities in the road corridor bylaw and Council's public safety and nuisance bylaw. Both of those have provisions in there that

regulate those kinds of activities around undertaking vehicle repairs and dumping of material on the roads. And Auckland Transport and Council do have mechanisms available to enforce breaches of these bylaws, which they can and do use, as has been mentioned. So I'll just hand over to my colleague, Sarah, and she'll just talk through a bit of what AT has been doing in terms of that enforcement.

Kia ora. My name's Sarah. So as Gary mentioned earlier, AT does do a lot of parking enforcement across the region, but also in the Waire and Manukau area. Our parking team have seen an increase in different behaviors over the last few years, including up to 2,000 or about 2,000 tickets for failing to park a large vehicle or a truck. So there does have to be a size difference there within the legal requirements for parking, and that can include warrant of fitness or failing to have a red light at the back of the vehicle.

And that has been over the past two months. We do note that approximately 90% of vehicles that are parked during the day are parked legally, and we are limited on what enforcement we can take when vehicles are parked legally. Obviously, we can't go around issuing tickets for things that don't exist, so we don't typically do that. And then when there are repairs that we have been identifying and that we have witnessed, we have either warned the operator or the driver, and we have referred those to the appropriate agency.

So whether that's internally for our teams or whether that's to council for environmental concerns. Mm-hmm. And finally, I'd like to reiterate that we do acknowledge the complaints and would love to work cohesively to get an outcome that meets everybody's requirements. Love to hear it. Thank you, and thank you to you both for making yourselves available this afternoon. I think we have some questions.

We've got 10 minutes with you for that. Let me just start. So what we are interested in is whether the purpose of the Land Transport Act is being realized by the practical outcome here. So looking at the complaint, on the face of it, it seems that there we don't have rules. As you say, Sarah, there's nothing illegal about the way that many of these trucks are parked, and yet what's being presented to us is that there are safety concerns, that there are environmental concerns, and those are things that the Land Transport Act

sets out to address. And so then we come down, Katie, as you've set out, there is a very broadly drafted bylaw here in 13 [3] , which allows the relevant authority to make those laws, and completely accepting that it will be different in different areas.

But the concern for this committee is whether the intention of Parliament here to make sure that the rules in place are for safety and for any negative environmental impacts and the mitigation of the public nuisance that might otherwise occur if rules aren't in place, is being thwarted by the lack of a rule.

So can you take us through, Katie, firstly, why is the bylaw drafted in this way, which is very, very open-ended? Almost you can imagine in a way that would not require any rules to be created under it. And then did AT in fact consider whether rules should be drafted that would deal with some of the issues that we're seeing 2,000 infringements and a range of other things that are not in fact illegal, but are still unsafe or create environmental

harm happening in a particular area. And if those things were considered, then why did rules not come about after those considerations? So I'll start off, and then I'll hand over to Sarah to answer some more of it from the Auckland Transport perspective.

I guess in terms of the reason why the bylaw is framed like that is really, as I mentioned, it's a more flexible approach that allows for AT and Council to be responsive to issues as they arise, and to create... Because obviously the process required for changing a bylaw itself takes a long time, right?

It's rightly a really robust process that takes a long time, and obviously for the really substantive regulation that is in the bylaw itself-Providing a flexible approach whereby council and AT can make resolutions and further prohibitions outside of the byelaw itself allows for that flexible, responsive, context-specific regulation where it arises and when it's needed.

The fact that it is empowering and doesn't require the making of resolutions in any kind of time-bound way doesn't mean that AT and council don't make resolutions under those clauses. And Sarah will probably be able to speak to this with greater detail, but there are resolutions that are made under those provisions. And Sarah, I don't know if you are in a position to speak to the process by which those come about, how they're raised, and the process that is gone through by the Traffic Control Committee to consider those when they come up.

And not just the process. Was it considered? Did anyone at the Traffic Control Committee consider, with the number of incidents that are not in fact illegal, but are in fact a problem, should we have a rule? Yes. So the Traffic Control Committee, they approve resolutions across the city. They also approve other things in other parts of legislation that relate to traffic controls, such as speed humps and traffic lights and lane layouts, and all great things like that that we need for our roads. We do thousands of them a week or a month.

We meet fortnightly, or TCC meets fortnightly. They've probably done close to 7,000 over the last couple of years. These are not big, long, lengthy special consultative procedures that we do. So we do that for the byelaw, and then when we get to the individual resolutions, it gives us a little bit more flexibility to say, "We're going to work within the controls that we consulted on under the byelaw,"

and then we make them for that area. So for some things, that might be time-restricted parking, it might be broken yellow lines, but we're working with the relevant people in the relevant areas. And so some local boards and some business associations are happy with paid parking. Some of them are not. Some of them want some speed humps to slow down the boy racers.

Some of them don't, and we can work with each of them individually, and that gives us the flexibility to tailor the result for each area or each set of people, depending on where we are. So what we might do in Orewa area might be very different from what we do in Parnell versus what we might do in, say, Pukekohe or Manukau, for example.

So, having the flexibility to do what we need to do across those areas in a shorter period of time means that if something is wrong, we can quickly go back and change those whilst working alongside developers and agencies where we need to. So the TCC's got a little bit of a robust process, but it's not just about this one particular byelaw. It covers a lot of other things that we're doing in that space.

Has the committee considered whether rules that have been suggested by the Wiri Business Association are required? We have the options to do certain things. It doesn't need to be in the byelaw, I think is what the TCC... Having the ability to do them under resolution rather than specified in a byelaw across the city means that whatever is put in there, then the whole city has to have it, and that wouldn't work for some areas.

Maybe they have. Okay, it's the final question. I'll hand over. Okay, we have one from Joseph Mooney. Just with the 2,000 infringements in the last nine months, helpful information for us, probably represents a relatively large sum for Auckland Transport. Do you know what that is?

I don't know because I don't know what the penalty is off by heart. I'm not a litigator or prosecutor unfortunately. No. No problem. So I don't have that one. I don't believe they're particularly large. They'll be somewhere between $50 and $150- Yeah... because they're primarily the large ones. Most of them are for failing to have a red light or failing to have a warrant of fitness or a COF, depending on the vehicle.

And if we do get a complaint during the day about a particular truck, then we'll come back at night and revisit that same truck if it's still there. So we are trying our best to proactively follow up on the complaints in a timely manner so that we can see them. Some of them are not-- So when we talk about rules, some of them are things that we can enforce, and some of them are just obligations.

So you have a duty to park safely. It's not a rule that you have to park safely. It's a little bit of a difference there. But you can't park within a certain number of meters from the intersection, which we can enforce. Okay. Joseph Mooney. Yeah. Thank you both. Useful to hear. And I'm interested in the public policy aspect of this, because we've heard from Gary that businesses can't effectively operate, can't access their businesses. And so my question is whether the byelaw is fit for purpose and whether your enforcement
agents can effectively do anything about that public policy issue, which does seem to be significant from what we heard from Gary.

I think to the extent that either Sarah or I can answer that as lawyers and not policy people, but I think the way that the byelaw is set up and framed to enable the making of resolutions and prohibitions under it, and separate from the byelaw itself, actually assists in that, so that when issues like businesses not being able to effectively operate arise, and where those issues are raised witAT or council, they can be looked at and consideration can be given as to whether a resolution restricting parking or putting in

yellow lines or whatever the mechanism might be, whether that is appropriate in the circumstance to deal with the issues that are being raised. Yeah. That's the central question, right? This is a very broadly drafted by-law. It's therefore a broadly applicable executive power. Our job is to work out whether it is in fact doing that, giving effect to-- There are trade-offs at play here between truck drivers and business owners.

And so whether in fact the decision-maker has considered that, and you've told me perhaps. And whether then the consideration did give effect to the primary purpose of the Land Transport Act, which includes safety and improvements on the environment and ensuring that there isn't a public nuisance aspect of that.

And we're still a little bit confused about that. Is there anything else that you can help us with in unpacking those two things? I think from my perspective, it really comes down to the complaints being raised with AT and council, whoever is the relevant authority for the particular road.

Because as the authority, if we're not aware of the issues, then we can't consider it. But where the issues are raised with us, then they should be considered. From our perspective as lawyers, then they should be considered. I can't, and I'm not sure if, Sarah, if you can speak to whether, the business association in this particular case, whether those concerns have been taken to the Traffic Control Committee for consideration.

But that is absolutely the intention for how this works. It's just we can only consider it when the issues are raised- Okay. Thank you... with the authority. Yeah. Thanks very much. Thank you both for your time. Thank you. Thank you for having us. Gary, we can't enter into a back and forth with you.
But as normal-
